narrative: I'm sorry to share that your law school application was rejected because our predictive model estimates a 63.9% chance you'll fail the bar exam on your first attempt, based on patterns from thousands of similar students. Your profile includes being in the bottom decile for grades in both your first and third undergraduate years—much lower than the typical student's middle-of-the-pack performance—along with a low LSAT score of 25.5 compared to the average of 36.7, an undergraduate GPA of 3.0 slightly below the norm of 3.21, upper-middle family income, male gender, Asian racial background, and plans to attend full-time. These details stood out when the model assessed your likelihood of bar success. The strongest factor pushing the prediction toward failure was your first-year grade decile, as being in the lowest group signals early academic struggles that often carry over. Right behind that was your third-year decile, similarly low and reinforcing concerns about consistent performance. Your LSAT score ranked third in influence, with its below-average result adding to the risk assessment. Family income came next, followed by racial background, undergraduate GPA, gender, and full-time status as the least influential—all nudging slightly toward a failure prediction compared to typical students. In contrast, students who pass the bar usually have stronger early grades, higher LSATs, and GPAs aligning with or exceeding averages. While demographics like yours are not uncommon, they didn't offset the academic red flags here. To improve your future chances, focus on rebuilding strong study habits and academic discipline, as your low deciles suggest areas for growth through targeted prep courses or tutoring. Retaking the LSAT to boost your score could help in reapplying, and dedicating time now to bar exam practice—perhaps via intensive review programs—might demonstrate readiness despite past hurdles. With effort in these changeable areas, you could shift the odds in your favor for another shot at admission. Keep pushing; many overcome early setbacks to succeed.
